description:
The pronghorn (pronunciation: /ˈprɒŋˌhɔːrn/)[3] (Antilocapra americana) is a species of artiodactyl mammal indigenous to interior western and central North America. Though not an antelope, it is often known colloquially in North America as the American antelope, prong buck, pronghorn antelope, or simply antelope[4] because it closely resembles the true antelopes of the Old World and fills a similar ecological niche due to parallel evolution.[5]

It is the only surviving member of the family Antilocapridae.[6] During the Pleistocene period, about 12 antilocaprid species existed in North America.[7] Three other genera (Capromeryx,[8][9] Stockoceros[10][11] and Tetrameryx[12]) existed when humans entered North America but are now extinct.

As a member of the superfamily Giraffoidea, the pronghorn's closest living relatives are the giraffes and okapi. The Giraffoidea are in turn members of the infraorder Pecora, making pronghorns more distant relatives of the Cervidae (deer) and Bovidae (cattle, goats, sheep, and antelopes), among others.

description:
Apparently the first Mono Basin record since the late 1990's (I checked with a number of long-time locals and biologists). 
There used to be a small population (<10) that summered around Mono Lake - especially northshore and Dechambeau Ranch area through the 80's/90's. I was told this herd in winter would move east and join up with the population that summered in the Bodie Hills. So the larger population wintered in Nevada which in spring would come west - splitting with some going into Bodie Hills, some coming to Mono Basin. But that small Mono Lake population apparently dried up by late 90's. 
This year Pronghorn became numerous around River Springs - I'd never seen them there before (15+ years of occasionally birding there)

description: @gpstewart and I are on a week-long birding/nature/photography trip to west Texas and adjacent New Mexico. After a couple of days in the Texas Panhandle we made it into the small town of Clayton in far n.e. New Mexico. Weather predictions for the night of Dec. 12 and all day Dec. 13 were for a strong cold front with sustained 35-40 mph winds with gusts to 60 mph, described as dangerous highway conditions with some high profile vehicles like tractor trailers that might blow over. We decided to stay all day the 13th in Clayton and stay off the highways till the weather calmed down a little. Sure enough, this morning (Dec. 13) winds were strong enough to make walking difficult and at 26 degrees and 40 mph winds it was interesting to say the least. Regardless of the weather, we decided to spend a few hours on small dirt roads north of Clayton out into the prairies to see what we could find as sitting in a motel all day was not too appealing to either of us. As expected, weather and blowing sand, blowing tumbleweeds, blowing small pebbles etc., made it interesting. Visited Clayton Lake State Park and had a few distant birds on the water such as 5 Common Mergansers, 3 Canada Geese etc., in protected coves but due to distance, etc., images will be marginal at best. At one point I tried to stand outside of the truck to take some shots and I almost had my camera ripped from my hands by the wind. So, anyway, today a day of marginal quality shots and tough weather. Hope to improve on both tomorrow. All days in nature are interesting, however!

description: While exploring Sully Springs last weekend (9-3-2023), I found these pronghorns along one of the Forest Service roads.  Pronghorns are not true antelope (a common name, though, as is the affectionate "speed goat") and their closest relatives are actually giraffes and okapi.  They're North America's fast land animal, capable of speeds upwards of 55mph (depending upon which source you research...  I'm not splitting hairs, for they're simply FAST!  lol).  They're also vigilant, for even though these were quite some distance away, they heard the shutter clicking (my "beep" is turned off but I was not using the electronic shutter at the time)  and I only had a few frames with which to work before the scene was filled with only badlands and pronghorn posterior.

Pronghorn (Antilocapra americana) is a native placental mammal in the Even-toed Ungulates and Cetaceans (Order Artiodactyla) and American Antelopes (Genus Antilocapra). Pronghorns are primarily found in grassland, sage scrub, chaparral, and desert habitats. The southern portion of their range consists mainly of arid grasslands and open prairies. 
Conservation Status: Vulnerable (S3) in California, US (NatureServe)

Pronghorn: California Department of Fish and Wildlife: Concise Species Summary and Rangemap: http://sibr.com/mammals/M182.html

Animal Diversity Web (ADW): https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Antilocapra_americana/
(Pronghorn) "Antilocapra americana is Endemic to North America and is distributed throughout the treeless plains, basins, and deserts of western North America, from the southern prairie provinces of Canada, southward into the western United States and to northern Mexico."

"Pronghorn gets its name from its horn sheaths that branch and have a forward-pointing tine, unlike the horns of species from the Ox (Bovidae) family." https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/42429-Antilocapra-americana
